column5: Thomas Böhmer‡PANGAEA‡"This data set presents the reconstructed vegetation cover for 1040 North American sites based on harmonized pollen data from the data set LegacyPollen 2.0. Sugita's REVEALS model (2007) was applied to all pollen records using REVEALSinR from the DISQOVER package (Theuerkauf et al. 2016). Pollen counts were translated into vegetation cover by accounting for taxon-specific pollen productivity and fall speed. Additionally
column6: relevant source areas of pollen were calculated using the aforementioned taxon-specific parameters and a Gaussian plume model for deposition and dispersal. Values for relative pollen productivity and fall speed from the synthesis from Wiezcorek and Herzschuh (2010) were updated with recent studies used to reconstruct vegetation cover. The average values from all Northern Hemisphere values were used where taxon-specific continental values were unavailable. As REVEALS was conceived to reconstruct vegetation from large lakes
column7: only records originating from large lakes (>= 50h) are marked as ""valid as site"" in the dataset. Reconstructions from other records can be used when spatially averaging several together. An example script to do so is provided on Zenodo (https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.12800290). Reconstructed tree cover was validated using modern Landsat remote sensing forest cover. Reconstructed tree cover has much lower errors than the original arboreal pollen percentages. Reconstructions of individual taxa are more uncertain. We present tables with reconstructed vegetation cover for all continents with original parameters. As further details
column8: we list a table with the taxon-specific parameters used
column10: and a list of parameters adjusted in the default version of REVEALSinR."‡PANGAEA; REVEALS; fossil pollen; paleoecology‡Dataset‡https://download.pangaea.de/dataset/963612/files/2025_North_America.csv‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡13.95191‡-162.21‡78.49167‡-50.4667‡

column8: Kristian Spilling‡PANGAEA‡The Baltic Sea has been affected by eutrophication for several decades with the result of a gradual increase of oxygen minimum zones at the sea floor and release of phosphate from sediments. This has generated a decreasing inorganic nitrogen:phosphorus (N:P) ratio and excess phosphate remaining after the spring bloom. In the present study
column9: a 17-days experiment was performed on June 2021 at the Tvärminne Research Station (Gulf of Finland
column10: Northern Baltic Sea) where the effects of the decreasing ratio of dissolved inorganic nitrogen and phosphorus on the planktonic community were investigated in 12 in-situ mesocosms. Temperature
column11: dissolved oxygen concentration and salinity were determined in situ
column12: whilst water samples were collected during the whole duration of the experiment using a Limnos sampler (Hydro-Bios
column13: Kiel)
column14: transported to the laboratory and processed for measurements of dissolved inorganic
column16: extracellular enzymatic activities
column18: bacterial abundance
column20: eukaryotic organisms and prokariotyc cell and filament counts. Dissolved inorganic nutrients (nitrate
column23: phosphate and silica) were determined according to Grasshoff et al. (1999). Concentrations of dissolved organic carbon and total dissolved nitrogen were determined. Dissolved organic nitrogen (DON) was calculated as difference between total dissolved nitrogen and dissolved inorganic nitrogen. Total dissolved phosphorus concentration was determined according to Koistinen et al. (2017). Dissolved organic phosphorus was calculated as difference between total dissolved phosphorus and dissolved inorganic phosphorus. Concentrations of particulate organic carbon (POC) and particulate organic nitrogen (PON) were also determined. The concentration of particulate organic phosphorus (POP) was measured according to Solórzano & Sharp (1980) with slight modification by Koistinen et al. (2017). Particulate organic elemental ratios POC:PON:POP were calculated on a molar basis. Extracellular enzymatic activities were determined according to Baltar et al. (2016). Bacterial production was determined as thymidine and leucine incorporations according to Fuhrman and Azam (1982)
column24: Smith and Azam (1992) and Kirchman et al. (1985). Chlorophyll-a was extracted from biomass according to Jespersen and Christoffersen (1987). Chlorophyll-a and Phycocyanin relative fluorescences were also measured. column2: and lipid composition of small pelagic fishes from Galicia and Southern Bay of Biscay (NE Atlantic
column6: Hilary G Close‡PANGAEA‡This dataset contains the values of natural abundance of stable carbon and nitrogen isotopes in bulk tissues
column7: carbon values in total lipid extracts and lipid fractions
column9: as well as the fatty acid profiles from the total lipid extracts and lipid fractions measured in 33 samples of 4 small pelagic fishes sampled during the PELACUS0322 cruise (NE Atlantic
column10: April 2022). Six capture sites were distributed over 4 sampling zones in Galicia and in adjacent waters of the southern Bay of Biscay. Length
column12: sex and maturity stage data for each individual analyzed are also provided. Fishes were collected by midwater trawls of ca. 30 m² pelagic net equipped with a graded-mesh netting (starting with 30 mm and ending with 4 mm). The duration of each trawl was between 25 and 40 min. Different species occurred in the same trawls. Samples of muscle tissue were preserved freeze-dried. No treatments for lipid removal were used prior to carbon analyses. Lipids were extracted from freeze‐dried aliquots using a modified version of the Bligh and Dyer (1959) method. Neutral and polar lipid fractions from the total lipid extracts were separated by adsorption chromatography on a silica gel micro-column (100–200 mesh
column14: pre-combusted silica gel) using solvents of different polarities. Stable isotope determinations (also carbon and nitrogen contents) were made by isotope-ratio mass spectrometry using an elemental analyzer (bulk samples and lipid extracts; Close et al. 2014; Bode et al. 2018) or a gas-chromatograph (derivatized amino acids; Bode et al. 2021) as the inlet device. column10: Anita Kirmer‡PANGAEA‡"This dataset provides standardized
column11: image-based estimates of percentage flower cover derived from the Grassworks-vegetation plots surveyed four times per year in parallel with pollinator assessments. Within 50 m × 5 m transects
column12: four 1 m × 1 m subplots were photographed vertically using RGB cameras. Approximately 12
column13: 000 images were geometrically corrected
column14: cropped to a standardized frame
column15: and merged into mosaics representing vegetation status per site and survey period. Mosaics were assigned artificial spatial references for processing in a GIS environment. Flower cover was quantified through supervised color classification using a Maximum Likelihood algorithm in ArcGIS Pro. Three flower color classes were defined: white
column16: yellow
column18: including transitional hues. Training data were compiled from randomly selected pixels of images across all sites and months
column19: covering a wide range of color shades
column21: and light conditions. Classification outputs were visually checked and iteratively refined. An attribute table was created to record
column23: whether flowers of the respective color category were present. Incorrectly classified flower coverings for a flower color not present in the image were set to zero. Percentage cover was calculated as the proportion of classified pixels per color class; total flower cover equals the sum of the three classes. Validation with flower-free control images indicated low false-positive rates (~0.4% mean total error)
column24: with comparable uncertainty expected for images containing flowers. The dataset includes image ID
column26: date

column31: with site coordinates reported at two-decimal precision (blurred for privacy)."‡Ecology & Environment; PANGAEA‡Dataset‡https://doi.pangaea.de/10.1594/PANGAEA.993515?format=textfile‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡48.22‡9.64‡54.37‡13.05‡Start Date: 2022-04-28 ; End Date: 2023-09-08

column14: Alix Vidal‡PANGAEA‡The dataset consists of a list of treatments and relative analyses done to determine different pathways through which live and dead earthworms can influence organic carbon stabilisation and inorganic carbon sequestration through mineral weathering. column15: with a mid-term sampling at 60 days to observe changes over time

column18: and earthworms were introduced either alive or dead. The data is separated into two datasets. The first dataset relates to the list of treatments and parameters measured to determine the effect of earthworms

column24: as alkalinity and major cations. The second dataset includes measurements on carbon dioxide emissions measured over the whole duration of the experiment for the different treatments. Data was collected within the BAM! project (Bio-Accelerated Mineral Weathering)

column8: Laurie C Hofmann‡PANGAEA‡"In the paper ""Salinity as a tool for strain selection in recirculating land-based production of Ulva spp. from germlings to adults"" (Cardoso et al. 2023) it was evaluated the impact of different nutrient sources on the relative growth rates (RELATIVE GROWTH RATE) of four different Ulva strains during their adult stage. column11: Germany) while the germlings (in the nursery set-up) were supplemented with half-strength Provasoli (PES; Provasoli 1968; modifications: HEPES-buffer instead of TRIS
column12: double concentration of Na₂Glycerophosphate; iodine enrichment following Tatewaki
column13: 1966). Therefore
column14: this experiment was performed to guarantee that the use of different nutrient sources would not impact the results of the former experiments. column15: Portugal in January 2021 and it was cultivated in laboratory conditions in the Alfred Wegener Institute (AWI)
column16: Bremerhaven
column17: Germany from that moment on. The Mediterranean strains belong to an AWI collection and were isolated in 1986 and 1987 (U. flexuosa and U. lacinulata
column18: respectively). The cultivation of the Mediterranean species with the purpose of using them for experiments started in June 2021. Fresh thalli from each species and population were placed into 1 L glass beakers and subjected to one of the two treatments: water enriched with commercial fertilizer Blaukorn or with half-strength Provasoli (n = 3). The fresh weight was measured once a week by collecting the seaweed and removing the excess water with absorbent paper three times before weighing the samples (Sartorius

column2: Ohad Peleg‡PANGAEA‡The Cape Rodney-Okakari Point Marine Reserve is New Zealand's oldest no-take marine reserve situated along the Leigh coast (est. 1978; the 'Leigh Marine Reserve' hereafter). Historic information on rocky reef communities in the Leigh Marine Reserve is available from baseline surveys in 1978 (Ayling 1978) and repeated in the 1990s (Babcock et al. 1999). While these provide valuable information on long-term changes in the reserve
column3: they did not include fished reefs outside the reserve that provide a control for the effect of protection. The monitoring program presented here was established in 1999 by N. Shears (University of Auckland) for the New Zealand Department of Conservation and includes four sites inside and four sites outside the Leigh Marine Reserve (Shears and Babcock 2003). At these sites
column4: the reefs are gradually sloping from the intertidal to the reef edge
column5: occurring at ~8–15 m depth. From 1999–2000 the reefs were surveyed in four depth ranges (<2
column8: but subsequent sampling has focussed on the 4-6 m depth range as this is representative of the depth range where sea urchins (Evechinus chloroticus) are most abundant and can form barren habitat (Shears & Babcock 2003; Shears et al. 2008). This depth range has been sampled sporadically (every 1–3 yr at these sites since 2001; note that only fished sites were surveyed in 2003). The surveys were conducted on SCUBA
column10: five 1-m2 quadrats were haphazardly sampled within the 4–6 m depth range. Within each quadrat
column11: the density (the number of individuals per metre square) of sea urchins and all large brown macroalgae were recorded (e.g. Ecklonia radiata
column12: Carpophyllum spp.
column13: Cystophora spp. and Sargassum sinclairii). For sea urchins
column14: behaviour was recorded as 'cryptic' (sea urchins found in holes
column15: cracks or crevices) or 'exposed' (sea urchins are out in the open and have a strong impact on macroalgal communities; Spyksma et al. 2017). For all large macroalgae the length of all fronds was measured to the nearest 5 cm using a measuring tape. For E. radiata
column16: measurements were taken for both stipe and total length (i.e. from the top of the holdfast to the meristem and the distal end of the primary lamina
column17: respectively). The percent cover of crustose coralline algae
column18: bare rock
column21: filamentous algae and sediment was assessed visually in each quadrat. column4: Anne F Sell‡PANGAEA‡Squids were collected during the research cruise SO285 on RV Sonne in the northern (nBUS) and southern (sBUS) Benguela Upwelling System between September 13 and October 8
column5: 2021 in order to determine their trophic position and nutritional ecology. The cruise was part of the TRAFFIC (Trophic tRAnsfer eFFICiency in the Benguela Current) project. Samples were retrieved from rectangular midwater trawl (RMT) net hauls at 8 stations in the nBUS (sampling ranged from surface down to 230–600 m) and 5 stations in the sBUS (sampling ranged from surface down to 500–750 m). Furthermore
column6: some squids were caught using hook and line in the upper two meters of the water column at 2 stations in the nBUS (ranging from 20 to 115 min per station
column7: 4–5 rods) and 4 stations in the sBUS (ranging from 45 to 105 min per station
column8: 4–6 rods). At 1 station in sBUS one squid was retrieved from a neuston catamaran haul (NCAT) used to sample the surface layer (0­–20 cm sampling depth). Dorsal mantle length (DML) of each specimen was measured to the nearest mm below. column1: https://doi.org/10.1594/PANGAEA.946550‡https://doi.org/10.1594/PANGAEA.946550‡https://doi.pangaea.de/10.1594/PANGAEA.946550‡https://doi.org/10.1594/PANGAEA.946550‡en‡doi:10.1594/PANGAEA.946550‡‡2022-07-22‡unrestricted‡Global crop calendars of maize and wheat in the framework of the WorldCereal project‡Juanma Cintas
column10: Sven Gilliams
column12: Zoltan Szantoi
column13: Alyssa Whitcraft‡PANGAEA‡Crop calendars provide valuable information on the timing of important stages of crop development such as the planting (SOS) and harvesting (EOS) dates. This information is critical for many crop monitoring applications such as crop type mapping
column14: crop condition monitoring
column15: and crop yield estimation and forecasting. Spatially detailed information on the crop calendars provides an important asset in this respect
column16: as it allows the algorithms to account for specific local circumstances while also maximizing their robustness and global applicability. Existing global crop calendar products
column17: as produced by the Group on Earth Observations' Global Agricultural Monitoring (GEOGLAM) Crop Monitor
column18: the United States Department of Agriculture Foreign Agricultural Service (USDA-FAS)
column20: and the European Commission Joint Research Centre's Anomaly hot Spots of Agricultural Production (ASAP)
column21: generally provide this information only at national or subnational level. In this work
column22: we present gridded SOS and EOS maps for wheat and maize that represent the crop calendars' spatial variability at 0.5° spatial resolution. These maps are generated in the framework of WorldCereal
column23: which is a European Space Agency (ESA) funded project whose cropland and crop type wheat and maize algorithms at global scale and at 10 m spatial resolution require this information. The proposed maps are built leveraging the above noted global products (Crop Monitor
column26: ASAP) whose datasets are combined into a baseline map and sampled to train a Random Forest algorithm based on climatic and geographic data. Their evaluation against test data from the baseline maps set aside for validation purposes show a good performance with SOS (EOS) R2 of 0.87 (0.92) and a Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 27 (26) days for wheat
column27: showing the lowest errors (RMSE < 15 days) in North America
column28: Central Europe
column31: all critical areas for global wheat production and trade. Meanwhile
column32: the largest errors (RMSE between 40 and 60 days) occurred in regions of South America close to the Amazon forest and in Africa close to the Congo Basin. In the case of maize
column33: the SOS (EOS) evaluation shows a R2 of 0.88 (0.79) and a RMSE of 24 (28) days for maize
column34: with the best performing regions (RMSE < 15 days) located in the Northern Hemisphere
column36: and Australia
column37: important areas for global maize production and trade. column25: and total root length was derived by multiplying specific root length by total root mass. Rao's quadratic entropy (FDQ) was calculated for each plot as an index of functional diversity

column11: relevant source areas of pollen were calculated using the aforementioned taxon-specific parameters and a gaussian plume model for deposition and dispersal. Values for relative pollen productivity and fall speed from the synthesis from Wiezcorek and Herzschuh (2010) were used for the reconstruction of vegetation cover. The average values from all Northern Hemisphere values were used where taxon-specific continental values were not available. This includes records of the Southern Hemisphere. We present tables with reconstructed vegetation cover for all continents with original parameters. As further details we list a table with the taxon-specific parameters used and a list of parameters adjusted in the default version of REVEALSinR.‡PANGAEA; REVEALS; fossil pollen; paleoecology‡Dataset‡https://doi.pangaea.de/10.1594/PANGAEA.961588?format=zip‡‡‡‡‡‡‡

column2: Ohad Peleg‡PANGAEA‡The Cape Rodney-Okakari Point Marine Reserve is New Zealand's oldest no-take marine reserve situated along the Leigh coast (est. 1978; the 'Leigh Marine Reserve' hereafter). Historic information on rocky reef communities in the Leigh Marine Reserve is available from baseline surveys in 1978 (Ayling 1978) and repeated in the 1990s (Babcock et al. 1999). While these provide valuable information on long-term changes in the reserve
column3: they did not include fished reefs outside the reserve that provide a control for the effect of protection. The monitoring program presented here was established in 1999 by N. Shears (University of Auckland) for the New Zealand Department of Conservation and includes four sites inside and four sites outside the Leigh Marine Reserve (Shears and Babcock 2003). At these sites
column4: the reefs are gradually sloping from the intertidal to the reef edge
column5: occurring at ~8–15 m depth. From 1999–2000 the reefs were surveyed in four depth ranges (<2
column8: but subsequent sampling has focussed on the 4-6 m depth range as this is representative of the depth range where sea urchins (Evechinus chloroticus) are most abundant and can form barren habitat (Shears & Babcock 2003; Shears et al. 2008). This depth range has been sampled sporadically (every 1–3 yr at these sites since 2001; note that only fished sites were surveyed in 2003). The surveys were conducted on SCUBA
column10: five 1-m2 quadrats were haphazardly sampled within the 4–6 m depth range. Within each quadrat
column11: the density (the number of individuals per metre square) of sea urchins and all large brown macroalgae were recorded (e.g. Ecklonia radiata
column12: Carpophyllum spp.
column13: Cystophora spp. and Sargassum sinclairii). For sea urchins
column14: behaviour was recorded as 'cryptic' (sea urchins found in holes
column15: cracks or crevices) or 'exposed' (sea urchins are out in the open and have a strong impact on macroalgal communities; Spyksma et al. 2017). For all large macroalgae the length of all fronds was measured to the nearest 5 cm using a measuring tape. For E. radiata
column16: measurements were taken for both stipe and total length (i.e. from the top of the holdfast to the meristem and the distal end of the primary lamina
column17: respectively). The percent cover of crustose coralline algae
column18: bare rock
column21: filamentous algae and sediment was assessed visually in each quadrat. column10: Raphael Morard‡PANGAEA‡The larger benthic foraminifera species Amphistegina lobifera has been successfully invading the Mediterranean Sea
column11: despite different temperature regimes from their original source location in the Red Sea. To investigate possible genetic (pre)adaptations in the invasive populations

column14: Marc Strous‡PANGAEA‡Groundwater ecosystems are globally widespread yet still poorly understood. column15: collected between 2015 and 2020. The study area comprised 14 major aquifers and a geographic area of ~210.000 km2. column7: Thomas Böhmer‡PANGAEA‡This data set presents the reconstructed vegetation cover for 1451 European sites based on harmonized pollen data from the data set LegacyPollen 2.0 and optimized RPP values. Sugita's REVEALS model (2007) was applied to all pollen records using REVEALSinR from the DISQOVER package (Theuerkauf et al. 2016). Pollen counts were translated into vegetation cover by taking into account taxon-specific pollen productivity and fall speed. Additionally
column8: relevant source areas of pollen were also calculated using the aforementioned taxon-specific parameters and a gaussian plume model for deposition and dispersal and forest cover was reconstructed. In this optimized reconstruction
column9: relative pollen productivity estimates for the ten most common taxa were first optimized by using reconstructed tree cover from modern pollen samples and LANDSAT remotely sensed tree cover (Sexton et al. 2013) for Europe. Values for non-optimized taxa for relative pollen productivity and fall speed were taken from the synthesis from Wiezcorek and Herzschuh (2020). The average values from all Northern Hemisphere values were used where taxon-specific continental values were not available. We present tables with optimized reconstructed vegetation cover for all records in Europe. As further details we list a table with the taxon-specific parameters used and a list of parameters adjusted in the default version of REVEALSinR.‡PANGAEA; REVEALS; fossil pollen; paleoecology‡Dataset‡https://download.pangaea.de/dataset/963618/files/composition_forest_opti_Europe.csv‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡31.41889‡-28.1697‡78.91667‡42.49694‡

column9: Yi Huang‡PANGAEA‡Dissolved rare earth elements (REEs) distribution in marginal sea is of significant importance to understand geochemical cycling of REEs in the ocean. column10: the largest marginal sea of the western Pacific Ocean. All seawater samples were filtered on board by using 0.45 mm membrane. (MilliPore Corp.). The results show that surface seawater REEs concentration are higher than the subsurface water indicating that surface seawater REEs distribution is likely impacted by terrigenous input through river discharges. column1: https://doi.org/10.1594/PANGAEA.963622‡https://doi.org/10.1594/PANGAEA.963622‡https://doi.pangaea.de/10.1594/PANGAEA.963622‡https://doi.org/10.1594/PANGAEA.963622‡en‡doi:10.1594/PANGAEA.963622‡‡2025-11-22‡unrestricted‡REVEALS reconstruction of past vegetation cover with optimized RPP values for samples in Australia and Oceania‡Laura Schild
column7: Thomas Böhmer‡PANGAEA‡This data set presents the reconstructed vegetation cover for 84 sites in Australia and Oceania based on harmonized pollen data from the data set LegacyPollen 2.0 and optimized RPP values. Sugita's REVEALS model (2007) was applied to all pollen records using REVEALSinR from the DISQOVER package (Theuerkauf et al. 2016). Pollen counts were translated into vegetation cover by taking into account taxon-specific pollen productivity and fall speed. Additionally
column8: relevant source areas of pollen were also calculated using the aforementioned taxon-specific parameters and a gaussian plume model for deposition and dispersal and forest cover was reconstructed. In this optimized reconstruction
column9: relative pollen productivity estimates for the ten most common taxa were first optimized by using reconstructed tree cover from modern pollen samples and LANDSAT remotely sensed tree cover (Sexton et al. 2013) for Australia and Oceania. Values for non-optimized taxa for relative pollen productivity and fall speed were taken from the synthesis from Wiezcorek and Herzschuh (2020). The average values from all Northern Hemisphere values were used where taxon-specific continental values were not available. We present tables with optimized reconstructed vegetation cover for all records in Australia and Oceania. As further details we list a table with the taxon-specific parameters used and a list of parameters adjusted in the default version of REVEALSinR.‡PANGAEA; REVEALS; fossil pollen; paleoecology‡Dataset‡https://download.pangaea.de/dataset/963622/files/composition_forest_opti_Indopacific.csv‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡-45.32‡-144.352‡20.79429‡107.7291‡

column6: Brian K Sorrell‡PANGAEA‡This dataset consists of downwelling and upwelling under ice PAR (photosynthetically available radiation; 400-700 nm wave band) and spectral irradiance data collected in March 2013 on the ice in Kangerlussuaq
column7: west Greenland. Under ice downwelling PAR and spectral irradiance in this area are well known
column8: but a lack of measurements of the upwelling irradiance was identified. It was hypothesised that this might add or enhance under ice primary production. Therefore this dataset was collected using PAR and spectral radiometer sensors both above and below the ice. Surface downwelling and upwelling PAR  (µmol photons/m**2/s)
column9: as well as albedo (0-1 scale) and air temperature (°C; 1 m above the snow surface)

Fifteen-meter radius sample plots (sites) were demarcated in the most homogeneous locations. Heterogeneity was accommodated by roughly assorting vegetation into one to two vegetation types per sampling plot. Within each area of roughly estimated vegetation types we selected one 0.5 x 0.5 m subplot for representative ground-layer above-ground biomass (ABG) harvesting (major taxa and other). For moss and lichen AGB harvesting inside 0.5 x 0.5 m subplots representative 0.1 x 0.1 m subplots were chosen. All ground-layer vegetation AGB assessments were calculated for the fifteen-meter radius plot in g/m² for each sample plot. The present trees and tree-shape growing shrub species were included only up to heights of <40 cm. column10: Inka Bartsch‡PANGAEA‡A survey of age class distribution and density of kelps was performed at Hansneset
column12: Svalbard
column13: at depths of 2.5
column14: 5 and 10m in June - August 2021. This dataset is part of a time series and complements the investigations conducted in 2013 (Bartsch et al.
column15: 2016; doi:doi:10.1007/s00300-015-1870-1). column2: Christian Möllmann‡PANGAEA‡This data contains monitoring data of the RV ALKOR cruise AL568b conducted in January 2022 (January 24th – February 1st) in the Western Baltic Sea by the University of Hamburg. The cruise was part of the 5-year monitoring programme Winter cod 2021-25 (Monitoring winter spawning activity of Western Baltic cod (Gadus morhua) (2021-25)) of the University of Hamburg aiming at investigating the early-winter spawning activity of Western Baltic cod to identify potential climate-induced shifts in spawning phenology. Stomachs were immediately frozen on -20°C and subsequently analysed in the laboratory of the University of Hamburg. Cod stomach samples were defrosted and adherent veins were carefully removed from the outer stomach tissue. column20: Koen Kramer‡PANGAEA‡This dataset stores measurements from two field campaigns in 20 environmental plantings aged 4–35 years old in Australia where stem diameters

column4: Jana S Petermann‡PANGAEA‡Urbanization affects ecological communities but urban ecology has mostly focused on large and charismatic species. Water-filled tree holes and other ephemeral small standing waters in cities constitute unique but inconspicuous breeding habitats for a range of insects. Their biodiversity is not well known and how their communities respond to increased urbanization in particular
column5: has rarely been studied. Using a Citizen Science Project
column6: we investigated how urbanization (measured as imperviousness
column7: human population density and altered temperature)
column9: electric conductivity) and detritus serving as a food source affected larval insect communities in artificial aquatic microhabitats. We found that these habitats were colonized quickly by a range of insect taxa. Their community abundance
column10: richness and decomposition rates were largely stable across different levels of urbanization. Fine detritus content increased larval abundance. Community composition shifted strongly with urbanization. The most abundant and frequent species in our study
column11: the exotic mosquito species Aedes japonicus
column12: responded negatively to imperviousness. Aquatic microhabitats could be shown to be important habitats for aquatic insects in cities. However
column13: their community composition may change with increased urbanization. As our results showed
column14: exotic species such as mosquitoes may dominate the communities in these habitats. In the case of vector species
column15: high abundances may affect human and animal health via increased pathogen transmission. Therefore
column16: we suggest raising awareness about potential risks of these habitats and possible measures preventing the establishment and spread of harmful species
column17: while still supporting native biodiversity in urban spaces.‡Citizen science; Field experiment; PANGAEA; artificial aquatic microhabitats; urbanization‡Dataset‡https://doi.pangaea.de/10.1594/PANGAEA.994214?format=textfile‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡47.628567‡12.9165‡48.068239‡13.634856‡Start Date: 2021-04-01 ; End Date: 2021-08-31

column21: Sara Beier‡PANGAEA‡Coastal marine environments — including lagoons
column23: and estuaries — are characterized by pronounced environmental variability
column26: and nutrient levels shaping microbial community structure and function. To explore these dynamic systems
column27: we sampled 26 sites between December 2021 and January 2023 along the coasts of France and Chile
column28: spanning lagoons
column32: and both coastal and offshore marine environments. Surface waters were collected using either Niskin bottles or polycarbonate carboys
column33: with additional deeper layers obtained at three sites. Monthly sampling at six French sites enables the assessment of seasonal dynamics. The presented dataset captures multiple environmental gradients
column34: ranging from terrestrial–marine transitions and salinity fluctuations to eutrophication level
column36: geographic and disturbance-related variation. A total of 116 samples were analysed for 13 environmental parameters alongside metabarcoding and metagenomic sequencing. Temperature and salinity were recorded in the field immediately after sampling. Chlorophyll a was quantified fluorometrically following acetone extraction (Holm-Hansen et al. 1965; Lorenzen 1967). Dissolved organic carbon (DOC) was measured using high-temperature catalytic oxidation (Benner and Strom 1993) with a TOC analyser. Fluorescent dissolved organic matter (fDOM) peaks T and C (as proxy for protein like and humic like DOM
column37: respectively) were quantified with a Jasco FP-8500 spectrofluorometer (Coble 1996). Leucine incorporation rates were determined via the centrifugation method (Smith and Azam 1992)
column38: and respiration rates were estimated via oxygen consumption in 5 ml glass vials using PreSens OxoDishes and a SensorDish® Reader. Inorganic nutrients were analyzed using a QuAAtro39 or AA3HR autoanalyzer (Grasshoff et al. 2009; Aminot and Kérouel 2007).‡Beach; Chilean Pacific coast; Coastal; Dissolved Organic Matter; Ecology & Environment; Eutrophication gradient; Fjord; Gulf of Lion; Harbor; Inorganic nutrients; Lagoon; Microbiology; PANGAEA; Patagonia; Productivity parameters; Salinity gradient; Saltmarsh; Seasonal survey; Terrestrial-marine transitions‡Dataset‡https://doi.pangaea.de/10.1594/PANGAEA.984582?format=textfile‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡-42.38651‡-72.955399‡43.11616663‡3.543333‡Start Date: 2021-12-02 ; End Date: 2024-01-17

column14: Marc Strous‡PANGAEA‡Groundwater ecosystems are globally widespread yet still poorly understood. We investigated the microbiology of >100 groundwater samples from 90 monitoring wells (<250m depth) located in 14 aquifers in the Canadian Prairie. This dataset contains relative abundances of archaeal lineages based on 16S rRNA gene amplicons retrieved from 52 groundwater samples of aquifers in the Canadian Prairie
column15: collected between 2015 and 2020. The study area comprised 14 major aquifers and a geographic area of ~210.000 km2. The goal of the study was to understand the links between the biogeochemistry and microbial ecology of groundwater ecosystems in diverse geological settings on a broad spatial scale. column6: Scarlett Trimborn‡PANGAEA‡This study highlights the importance of manganese (Mn) next to iron (Fe) for the growth of specific Southern Ocean phytoplankton groups with important implications for carbon export in the Weddell Sea. A Fe-Mn bottle amendment experiment and an aggregation experiment were performed with a natural phytoplankton community during Polarstern expedition PS124 in 2021 in the Weddell Sea. At this location
column7: seawater was pumped (using trace metals clean techniques) from 25 m depth to fill polycarbonate bottles. The Control treatment consist of the sampled seawater without any trace metals addition
column8: while the other three treatments were enriched with either FeCl₃ alone (0.5 nM; +Fe treatment) or MnCl₂ alone (1 nM; +Mn treatment) or both trace metals together (+FeMn treatment). All treatments were done in triplicate 2.5 L PC bottles. After on average 7 days
column10: particulate organic carbon and primary production were taken to detect FeMn co-limitation effect on species composition
column11: primary production and carbon export. Results showed that when Fe and Mn were added together
column12: primary production rates were highest due to an increased abundance of the prymnesiophyte Phaeocystis antarctica. This change within the phytoplankton community led to highly carbon-enriched aggregates and a four-fold increase in the carbon export potential compared to the doubling in the only Fe treatment. Our findings highlight that even small changes in plankton community composition can have significant effects on the carbon export potential of the Southern Ocean
column13: a region of critical importance for anthropogenic carbon dioxide drawdown.‡Aggregation; Laboratory experiment; PANGAEA; Phytoplankton; RV Polarstern; Weddel Sea; carbon export; iron; manganese‡Dataset‡https://doi.pangaea.de/10.1594/PANGAEA.957278?format=textfile‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡-76.09976‡-30.38305‡-76.09531‡-30.30265‡Start Date: 2021-02-28 ; End Date: 2021-02-28

column10: Marco Milazzo‡PANGAEA‡An in situ reciprocal transplant experiment was carried around a volcanic CO2 vent (Vulcano Island
column12: Italy) to evaluate the anti-predator responses of an anemone goby species exposed to ambient (∼380 μatm) and high (∼850 μatm) CO2 sites during two sampling surveys on May 17th – June the 2nd 2019
column13: and 9–18 September 2019. Behavioral observations followed a well-established protocol adopted previously for other fish species
column14: which consisted in recording gobies responses before and after the presentation of a predator (i.e. predator stimulus). Specifically
column15: for each replicate a 4-min pre-stimulus presentation period was followed by a 4-min post-stimulus presentation period during which each fish was exposed to the visual and olfactory cues of an adult painted comber S. scriba. During the pre- and the post-stimulus periods we measured:  the activity level
column16: as the amount of time (sec) spent by the fish swimming in the entire experimental compartment; (ii) the time spent (sec) by the fish actively swimming or resting at <20 cm from the predator compartment (i.e. the predator zone) and at >20 cm from the predator compartment (no predator zone); (iii) the shelter use
column17: as the total time the fish spent near the anemone (i.e. less than 5 cm); (iv) the minimum approach distance

column20: Koen Kramer‡PANGAEA‡This dataset stores measurements from two field campaigns in 20 environmental plantings aged 4–35 years old in Australia where stem diameters
column22: and heights of individual trees and shrubs were collected
column23: and above- and below-ground woody biomass (AGB
column24: BGB) were calculated. The first measurement was undertaken by The University of Melbourne in collaboration with Land Life Company between March and June 2022 covering 14 sites aged 21-35 years in Victoria
column25: Australia. The purpose of this field campaign was to re-evaluate the biomass carbon stock of sites previously assessed in 2000 by CSIRO within the stem diameter database (SDD; Paul et al. 2020). For each site
column26: to ensure representative coverage
column27: the sampling area was divided into equal-sized sectors and within each sector a random transect of 0.05 ha (100 m length by 5 m width) was laid out based on a restricted random sampling design. Two to five transects were measured per site
column28: depending on either CSIRO last measurement plots or to optimize for the project's resources. In total
column29: the dataset contains 48 transects and 4703 unique tree or shrub measurements. Stem diameters were measured with a diameter tape (>10 cm) or a Stepped diameter Gauge (<10 cm)
column30: and height (measured with a laser hypsometer) and crown radius (estimated using a measuring tape) were assessed for only a few representative trees (selected to encompass the full diameter range). The second field campaign was undertaken by Land Life Company between June and July 2023 covering six environmental planting sites aged 4-23 years in Victoria
column31: Australia. The purpose of this campaign was to collect crown data in two directions (perpendicular and parallel to the seeding line). For each site
column32: 14-20 transects of 20 m length were measured (following the seeding line and avoiding gaps or areas of dead plants). In total
column33: the data contains 108 transects and 1341 unique tree and shrub measurements. Crown radius was measured with a tape for each tree or shrub and in addition
column34: stem diameter (measured with a caliper) and height (measured with a laser hypsometer) were also collected. For both datasets
column36: stem diameters of multi-stem trees and shrubs were grouped into a single diameter equivalent using the equation of Penman et al. (2003)
column37: and then specific plant functional type (PFT) allometric equations (Paul et al. 2015
column38: Paul et al. 2018) were used to derive above and below-ground biomass.‡Carbon capture projection; Crown radius; Environmental plantings; Field experiment; Model accuracy; PANGAEA; Revegetation; Woodland restoration; Woody biomass; carbon sequestration; productivity‡Dataset‡https://doi.pangaea.de/10.1594/PANGAEA.972726?format=zip‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡-37.2487‡141.68171‡-35.92217‡146.1436‡Start Date: 2022-03-18 ; End Date: 2023-07-19

column7: Thomas Böhmer‡PANGAEA‡This data set presents the reconstructed vegetation cover for 1132 North American sites based on harmonized pollen data from the data set LegacyPollen 2.0 optimized RPP values. Sugita's REVEALS model (2007) was applied to all pollen records using REVEALSinR from the DISQOVER package (Theuerkauf et al. 2016).  Pollen counts were translated into vegetation cover by taking into account taxon-specific pollen productivity and fall speed. Additionally
column8: relevant source areas of pollen were also calculated using the aforementioned taxon-specific parameters and a gaussian plume model for deposition and dispersal and forest cover was reconstructed. In this optimized reconstruction
column9: relative pollen productivity estimates for the ten most common taxa were first optimized by using reconstructed tree cover from modern pollen samples and LANDSAT remotely sensed tree cover (Sexton et al. 2013) for North America. Values for non-optimized taxa for relative pollen productivity and fall speed were taken from the synthesis from Wiezcorek and Herzschuh (2020). The average values from all Northern Hemisphere values were used where taxon-specific continental values were not available. We present tables with optimized reconstructed vegetation cover for all records in North America. As further details we list a table with the taxon-specific parameters used and a list of parameters adjusted in the default version of REVEALSinR.‡PANGAEA; REVEALS; fossil pollen; paleoecology‡Dataset‡https://download.pangaea.de/dataset/963619/files/composition_forest_opti_North_America.csv‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡13.95191‡-162.21‡78.49167‡-50.4667‡

column5: Thomas Böhmer‡PANGAEA‡"This data set presents the reconstructed vegetation cover for 2773 sites based on harmonized pollen data from the data set LegacyPollen 2.0 (https://doi.pangaea.de/10.1594/PANGAEA.965907). 1040 sites are located in North America
column7: and 446 in Asia. Sugita's REVEALS model (2007) was applied to all pollen records using REVEALSinR from the DISQOVER package (Theuerkauf et al. 2016). Pollen counts were translated into vegetation cover by accounting for taxon-specific pollen productivity and fall speed. Additionally
column8: relevant source areas of pollen were calculated using the aforementioned taxon-specific parameters and a Gaussian plume model for deposition and dispersal. Values for relative pollen productivity and fall speed from the synthesis from Wiezcorek and Herzschuh (2010) were updated with recent studies used to reconstruct vegetation cover. The average values from all Northern Hemisphere values were used where taxon-specific continental values were unavailable. As REVEALS was conceived to reconstruct vegetation from large lakes
column9: only records originating from large lakes (>= 50h) are marked as ""valid as site"" in the dataset. Reconstructions from other records can be used when spatially averaging several together. An example script to do so is provided on Zenodo (https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.12800290). Reconstructed tree cover was validated using modern Landsat remote sensing forest cover. Reconstructed tree cover has much lower errors than the original arboreal pollen percentages. Reconstructions of individual taxa are more uncertain. We present tables with reconstructed vegetation cover for all continents with original parameters. As further details
column10: we list a table with the taxon-specific parameters used
column12: and a list of parameters adjusted in the default version of REVEALSinR."‡PANGAEA; REVEALS; fossil pollen; paleoecology‡Dataset‡https://doi.pangaea.de/10.1594/PANGAEA.974798?format=zip‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡13.95191‡-170.248‡78.91667‡44.47‡

column5: Möller Fenja-Marie‡PANGAEA‡Samples were collected between 03/26/2018 and 04/27/2018 around the northern tip of the Antarctic Peninsula (63° 0' 1.843'' S
column6: 60° 0' 16.901''W) onboard the RV Polarstern during the PS112 campaign in order to identify the elemental composition and stoichiometry of the Antarctic krill (Euphausia superba) and salps (Salpa thompsoni). The sampling stations were situated in the survey grid of the Antarctic Marine Living Resources Program (AMLR). At the time of sampling
column7: the study area was ice free and ambient Chlorophyll a levels were comparable to previous studies in this time frame (e.g. Schofield et al.
column8: 2017). The phytoplankton community was dominated by dinoflagellates
column10: which is typical for autumn (March–May) around the NAP (Pauli et al.
column11: 2021). Krill and salps were sampled by using the 1.8 m² Isaacs-Kidd Midwater Trawl Net (IKMT) equipped with a 505 μm mesh which is suitable to collect both salps and krill in good condition. The net was towed obliquely to 170 m
column12: or 20 m from the bottom
column13: at a speed of 2 kts. From these tows
column14: we collected 162 adult krill and 121 blastozooid salp individuals on board of which we analyzed 140 krill and 108 salp samples for their elemental content (total organic carbon
column15: nitrogen and phosphorus). Size in mm (using graph paper
column16: Seibert publisher) and stage of krill and salp individuals were determined on board before further processing. For size measurements of S. thompsoni
column17: we used the oral atrial length. Total length of Antarctic krill was measured from the anterior margin of the eye to the tip of the telson
column18: excluding the terminal spine. Prior to the elemental analysis
column19: the digestive system of krill and salps was removed to avoid contamination of the tissue elemental composition by the elemental signature of the gut content. Salp individuals were immediately dissected on board after size measurements and frozen at -20 °C. Krill individuals were frozen on board and dissected later in the lab. Frozen krill and salp individuals were individually ground prior to the analyses using a pebble mill and homogenized with distilled water. Homogenization enabled us to take sub-samples of single individuals to analyze the total carbon
column21: and phosphorus composition of the same specimen. column7: Thomas Böhmer‡PANGAEA‡This data set presents the reconstructed vegetation cover for 164 African sites based on harmonized pollen data from the data set LegacyPollen 2.0 and optimized RPP values. Sugita's REVEALS model (2007) was applied to all pollen records using REVEALSinR from the DISQOVER package (Theuerkauf et al. 2016).  Pollen counts were translated into vegetation cover by taking into account taxon-specific pollen productivity and fall speed. Additionally
column8: relevant source areas of pollen were also calculated using the aforementioned taxon-specific parameters and a gaussian plume model for deposition and dispersal and forest cover was reconstructed. In this optimized reconstruction
column9: relative pollen productivity estimates for the ten most common taxa were first optimized by using reconstructed tree cover from modern pollen samples and LANDSAT remotely sensed tree cover (Sexton et al. 2013) for Africa. Values for non-optimized taxa for relative pollen productivity and fall speed were taken from the synthesis from Wiezcorek and Herzschuh (2020). The average values from all Northern Hemisphere values were used where taxon-specific continental values were not available. We present tables with optimized reconstructed vegetation cover for all records in Africa. As further details we list a table with the taxon-specific parameters used and a list of parameters adjusted in the default version of REVEALSinR.‡PANGAEA; REVEALS; fossil pollen; paleoecology‡Dataset‡https://download.pangaea.de/dataset/963620/files/composition_forest_opti_Africa.csv‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡-46.8925‡-24.7026‡37.15294‡59.66694‡

column5: Maren Striebel‡PANGAEA‡To investigate the impact of varying rainfall and run-off conditions on a freshwater plankton community
column6: we conducted an in-situ mesocosm experiment at Lake Erken in Sweden. Three distinct sets of nutrient pulses
column10: were selected as experimental scenarios. Lake Erken is a large mesotrophic to eutrophic lake and part of the Swedish SITES AquaNet mesocosm and monitoring network (Urrutia-Cordero et al. 2021). The mesocosm system at the Erken Laboratory consists of 16 polyethylene cylinders with a depth of 1.5 meters and a diameter of 0.8 meters attached to a floating platform. On the morning of the 6th of July 2022
column11: the mesocosms were carefully filled with lake water from 1.5 m distance from the edge of the platform by using a gasoline pump (3HP
column12: MecTools). The experimental manipulations started one day after filling the mesocosms. The experimental treatments differed in the frequency/intensity (from one extreme pulse to small daily pulses) and chronology (regular or irregular temporal pattern) of the nitrate
column13: phosphate and dissolved organic carbon (DOC) pulses and encompassed the following treatments: (i) regular daily pulses
column14: (ii) multiple irregular pulses with variable amounts
column15: (iii) one extreme pulse and (vi) an unenriched control. The three nutrient pulse treatments received the same total amount of additions at the end of the simulated rainfall period (20 days) which was followed by a 17-day recovery period. Each treatment was run with a replication of 4. Homogenized water samples for size-fractionated stoichiometric analyses have been taken from each mesocosm using a Ruttner water sampler every fourth day which resulted in 10 data points (6 in the simulated rainfall period and 4 in the recovery period). The water sample from each mesocosm was separated into two size classes (<105 µm and >105 µm) and filtered onto one filter for particulate organic carbon and nitrogen (POC/PON) and one filter for particulate organic phosphorus (POP)
column16: respectively
column17: using pre-combusted and acid-washed 0.45 µm glass microfiber filters (Whatman
column18: GF/C). Additionally
column19: filters for biogenic silicate (BSi) have been taken for the smaller size fraction (Whatman NC45 membrane filters
column22: 25 mm diameter; CAT no 10401106). column4: Lukas Lehnert‡PANGAEA‡The broad importance of land use and land cover information has been defined by and confirmed for many applications. Therefore
column5: many land cover products have been developed at various scales (i.e.
column10: and global). Several studies have reported inconsistencies among global land cover (GLC) products causing that the accuracy of these products differ between regions. Recently
column11: this issue has received a new level of attention
column12: because many studies have pointed out that the inaccuracy of land cover products at regional scale can make a huge impact on the results of other applications relying on the GLC products (in the following downstream applications). Therefore
column13: developing a method which can be easily and quickly applied to many different regions
column14: but produce highly accurate land cover information is of utmost importance. To meet the first two criteria
column15: several studies successfully used existing GLC products to automatically generate samples. However
column16: none of these studies have been focused on the quality of the samples
column17: which directly and largely affect the classification results. In this context
column18: and taking Mongolia as a case study
column21: and accurate method to produce annual land cover maps with 250 m spatial resolution for entire Mongolia over a period of 20 years
column22: from 2001 to 2020. The maps are based on MODIS data (products MOD13Q1 and MCD12Q1
column23: version 6) and produced using modern machine learning techniques (the Random Forest) on the Google Earth Engine. Training samples have been selected by developing a semi-random approach which ensures that samples are spatially well-distributed
column24: the number of samples for each class is in a similar order irrespective of the dominance of the land-cover classes and the samples are sufficiently apart from each other to reduce spatial autocorrelation. It is worth noting that we have selected Mongolia because of the low accuracy of GLC in this vast and remote country. Our results show that the accuracy of the new land cover maps improved compared with the corresponding MODIS products and if visually compared to Landsat images acquired at the same time. Overall accuracy from the validation data was approximately 90% for all new maps compared to 75% for the existing MODIS product. column2: Effrosyni Fatira‡PANGAEA‡"Macroecological patterns of life history traits
column4: are fundamental to understanding the biogeography and diversity of marine invertebrates. The present dataset provides curated information on carapace length (CL) of the first larval instar of decapod crustaceans. Data were obtained from published descriptions in which the morphology of the larva was illustrated using drawings or photographs. We extracted the CL data measuring from the anterior margin (or the base of the rostral spine when present) to the posterior dorsal margin of the carapace. We used our own collection of published taxonomic descriptions to gather data regarding ""number of larval stages"" and ""size of the first larval instar"". To expand the dataset
column5: published information was added from the Institute for Scientiﬁc Information Web of Knowledge using the search terms ""larva"" AND ""development"" AND ""Decapoda"" AND ""description"". Moreover
column9: etc.) were individually searched for extra datasets. We were careful to include only studies following the taxonomic standard propositions by Clark et al. (1998) updated by Clark and Cuesta (2015). To avoid misidentifications
column10: we only used descriptions based on larvae obtained from laboratory-reared ovigerous decapods or collected in the field and identified by DNA barcoding. Moreover
column11: those descriptions with low quality drawings/photographs
column12: and/or without scale were discarded. Following these criteria
column13: we screened the abstracts and figures to select appropriate studies. In some cases
column14: we extracted carapace size data directly from published tables or scatter plots using the free online app PlotDigitalizer (https://plotdigitizer.com). Our dataset of larval size includes a total of 645 species across Decapoda: 47 Achelata

column7: Priyeshu Srivastava‡PANGAEA‡We provide a dataset of 40Ar/39Ar age constraints for seven samples of sedimentary deposits recovered from a 120 m deep borehole drilled in the city of Rome in the year 2022. Twenty to thirty sanidine crystals were separated from the sandy matrix of the gravel beds obtained from the borehole. Each gravel bed is part of an aggradational succession deposited in response to sea-level rise in the delta of the Paleo-Tiber River during the late Lower Pleistocene. These aggradational successions are characterized by a sharp boundary that separates a layer of well-rounded limestone and chert pebbles
column8: with diameters reaching up to approximately 10 cm
column9: embedded in a matrix of silty sand (0.5-1.0 mm). This layer is distinct from another layer of mostly clayey (<0.004 mm) and sandy (<0.5 mm) sediments that is several meters thick. Based on the conceptual model of aggradational successions proposed by Marra et al. (2016
column11: the sharp sedimentary boundaries are used as a proxy for glacial terminations. The achieved geochronologic constraints allow for an overall good correlation between each aggradational succession
column12: represented by the basal coarse gravel abruptly transitioning to sandy clay sediments
column13: and each period of sea-level rise inferred from the d18O curve in the interval encompassing MIS 19 through MIS 37

column8: Laurie C Hofmann‡PANGAEA‡"In the paper ""Salinity as a tool for strain selection in recirculating land-based production of Ulva spp. from germlings to adults"" (Cardoso et al. 2023) the relative growth rates of germlings of different Ulva species and strains were measured to evaluate the impact of different salinity treatments. column9: Klaus Jürgens‡PANGAEA‡Upwelling systems are significant sources of atmospheric nitrous oxide (N₂O). The Benguela Upwelling System is one of the most productive regions worldwide and a temporally variable source of N₂O. Strong O₂ depletions above the shelf are favoring periodically OMZ formations. column2: Whooper Swan and Bewick's swan) in the eastern part of the Gulf of Finland based on fecal analysis‡Sergei Kouzov
column5: Anna Kravchuk‡PANGAEA‡The quality of swans' nutrition at spring migration stopovers is important for their successful breeding. The aim of this study was to increase our understanding of the differences in nutrition of different swan species when sharing the same habitat. Microscopic analysis of Cygnus olor
column7: and C. bewickii feces collected in the eastern part of the Gulf of Finland in February-April 2014-2019 was performed. We measured food preferences of the three swan species using non-metric multidimensional scaling (NMDS). The width and overlap of dietary niches were also calculated. The diet of C. olor consists almost entirely of soft submerged aquatic vegetation
column8: mainly macroalgae. Samples of the other two species except macroalgae contained large amounts of young shoots and roots of rigid semi-submerged and coastal vegetation. The dietary niche of C. cygnus is the most isolated because it is dominated by thick rhizomes of Phragmites australis
column9: which are hardly used by other swan species. The diet of Bewick's swans was similar in many respects to that of the Mute swan
column10: but Bewick's swans much more often preferred vegetative parts of submerged and semi-submerged plants
column12: Potamogeton perfoliatus
column13: Sparganium sp.

column4: except in the coastal station S1 of Maz V where a sample was collected at the mixed layer); base of the oxycline (at 60–125 m depth); OMZ core (only for Maz IV at 250 m depth); and bottom (between 23 m depth in S1 and 670 m depth in S4). column5: Merck Millipore). Genomic DNA was extracted with the DNeasy PowerWater Kit (Qiagen). The V3–V4 region of the 16S rRNA gene was amplified using the index and adaptor-linked primers Bakt_341F and Bakt_805R

The experiments were conducted as before-after-control-impact studies (BACI) exclusively in habitat type fine and medium sands with ripple structure (a sufficiently large lanice field could not be sampled). column6: Schleswig-Holstein and Denmark. For this purpose
column8: methods were developed to represent small-scale differences in fishing effort using satellite data in tidal flat systems. column9: Ulrike Herzschuh‡PANGAEA‡Field investigations were performed selecting locations to include the variety of present boreal forest stands between 113-130 °E
column10: eastwards of the city Yaktusk until westernmost sites close to Lake Khamra. To provide a full assessment of the current state of aboveground biomass the ground vegetation layer was additionally sampled. Fifteen-meter radius sample plots (sites) were demarcated in the most homogeneous locations. Heterogeneity was accommodated by roughly assorting vegetation into one to two vegetation types per sampling plot. Within each area of roughly estimated vegetation types we selected one 0.5 x 0.5 m subplot for representative ground-layer above-ground biomass (ABG) harvesting (major taxa and other). For moss and lichen AGB harvesting inside 0.5 x 0.5 m subplots representative 0.1 x 0.1 m subplots were chosen. All ground-layer vegetation AGB assessments were calculated for the fifteen-meter radius plot in g/m² for each sample plot. The present trees and tree-shape growing shrub species were included only up to heights of <40 cm. column11: is situated in the northernmost sector of the Benguela Upwelling System or BUS (Southeastern Atlantic Ocean). The BUS region is interested by several water masses
column12: among which the Benguela Oceanic Current (BOC) and the South Atlantic Central Waters (SACWs). The BOC represents a cold surface water mass flowing along the coast of Africa

column14: instead

column19: causing the shift of the Angola-Benguela thermal front (ABF). Furthermore
column20: warm waters from the Indian Ocean can access the BUS through a mechanism known as the Agulhas Leakage. The collected dataset was used to better understand variations in the local paleoceanographic conditions (stability of the thermocline) induced by the interactions of the previously mentioned water masses. column22: the Globorotalia truncatulinoides dextral/sinistral coiling ratio
column23: as well as the Agulhas Leakage Efficiency (ALE) Index were applied to characterize the different water masses as well as to reconstruct their interplay during the Early-Middle to Late Pleistocene. column7: Thomas Böhmer‡PANGAEA‡This data set presents the reconstructed vegetation cover for 706 Asian sites based on harmonized pollen data from the data set LegacyPollen 2.0 and optimized RPP values. Sugita's REVEALS model (2007) was applied to all pollen records using REVEALSinR from the DISQOVER package (Theuerkauf et al. 2016). Pollen counts were translated into vegetation cover by taking into account taxon-specific pollen productivity and fall speed. Additionally
column8: relevant source areas of pollen were also calculated using the aforementioned taxon-specific parameters and a gaussian plume model for deposition and dispersal and forest cover was reconstructed. In this optimized reconstruction
column9: relative pollen productivity estimates for the ten most common taxa were first optimized by using reconstructed tree cover from modern pollen samples and LANDSAT remotely sensed tree cover (Sexton et al. 2013) for Asia. Values for non-optimized taxa for relative pollen productivity and fall speed were taken from the synthesis from Wiezcorek and Herzschuh (2020). The average values from all Northern Hemisphere values were used where taxon-specific continental values were not available. We present tables with optimized reconstructed vegetation cover for records in Asia. As further details we list a table with the taxon-specific parameters used and a list of parameters adjusted in the default version of REVEALSinR.‡PANGAEA; REVEALS; fossil pollen; paleoecology‡Dataset‡https://download.pangaea.de/dataset/963617/files/composition_forest_opti_Asia.csv‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡18.377‡-170.248‡75.33333‡44.47‡

column2: the genetic basis of seed or seedling sourcing is a controversial issue. Formerly
column3: non-local seed sourcing of naturally occurring herbaceous species was common practice. Lately local provenancing got wide attention
column4: and additional strategies are being recommended. All of these
column6: raise the costs for restoration efforts. Here we test whether the earlier introduction of non-local seeds of Leucanthemum vulgare agg. in the course of ecological restoration can still be detected several years after the measure. The results are used to provide conservation recommendations for this widespread herbaceous insect-pollinated species. We analyzed the population genetic pattern (AFLP) of the ox-eye daisy in Central Germany on sites formerly restored with non-local seed sources (R) and compared these to the ones of indigenous populations (I). All populations of Leucanthemum vulgare agg. were genetically diverse and did not clearly distinguish between R and I sites. Furthermore
column7: no clear evidence of distinct local genetic patterns was observed. Based on our results
column8: we argue for the use of non-native seeds in the course of restoration measures for ox-eye daisies due to cost savings
column9: but support the demand for a broader population genetic monitoring in order to put the entire system of seed provenance on a solid empirical basis.‡AFLP; PANGAEA‡Dataset‡https://doi.pangaea.de/10.1594/PANGAEA.955709?format=textfile‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡50.219039‡8.556461‡51.220721‡10.415881‡

column7: Thomas Böhmer‡PANGAEA‡This data set presents the reconstructed vegetation cover for 191 South American sites based on harmonized pollen data from the data set LegacyPollen 2.0 and optimized RPP values. Sugita's REVEALS model (2007) was applied to all pollen records using REVEALSinR from the DISQOVER package (Theuerkauf et al. 2016). Pollen counts were translated into vegetation cover by taking into account taxon-specific pollen productivity and fall speed. Additionally
column8: relevant source areas of pollen were also calculated using the aforementioned taxon-specific parameters and a gaussian plume model for deposition and dispersal and forest cover was reconstructed. In this optimized reconstruction
column9: relative pollen productivity estimates for the ten most common taxa were first optimized by using reconstructed tree cover from modern pollen samples and LANDSAT remotely sensed tree cover (Sexton et al. 2013) for South America. Values for non-optimized taxa for relative pollen productivity and fall speed were taken from the synthesis from Wiezcorek and Herzschuh (2020). The average values from all Northern Hemisphere values were used where taxon-specific continental values were not available. We present tables with optimized reconstructed vegetation cover for all records in South America. As further details we list a table with the taxon-specific parameters used and a list of parameters adjusted in the default version of REVEALSinR.‡PANGAEA; REVEALS; fossil pollen; paleoecology‡Dataset‡https://download.pangaea.de/dataset/963621/files/composition_forest_opti_South_America.csv‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡-54.8707‡-89.48‡10.7062‡-36.345‡

column5: Gastón M Díaz‡PANGAEA‡The dataset comprises a Canopy Height Model (CHM) derived from UAV-LiDAR surveys of a native Nothofagus forest in Patagonia. Data acquisition was conducted during the 2024 austral summer (leaf-on season) using a YellowScan® Surveyor Ultra LiDAR system
column6: equipped with a Hesai XT32M2X scanner and mounted on a DJI® Matrice 300 RTK quadcopter. The laser scanner operates at a wavelength of 903 nm
column8: 000 pulses per second and recording up to three echoes per pulse
column9: with a system accuracy of approximately ±3 cm. Survey flight parameters were as follows: a mean flying altitude of 100 m above ground level
column10: a flight speed of 5 m s⁻¹
column11: and a 70° field of view. The average point density achieved was 178 ppsm
column12: utilizing a simple grid pattern with a 60% swath overlap and 60 m spacing between adjacent flight lines. Post-processing of the trajectories was performed using Applanix POSPac software to generate Smooth Best Estimated Trajectory (SBET) files
column13: integrated with base station data from a GNSS Spectra SP60 L1/L2 receiver positioned over a known geodetic point. Subsequently
column14: YellowScan CloudStation software was employed for strip adjustment and the classification of terrain and non-terrain points. A Digital Terrain Model (DTM) and a Digital Surface Model (DSM) were generated at a spatial resolution of 0.5 m. The CHM was then calculated as the algebraic difference between the DSM and the DTM. Finally
column15: RStudio (version 2025.09.2+418) was utilized to filter outliers exceeding 50 m in height — reclassifying such values as N/A — and to reproject the resulting CHM to the WGS 84 geographic coordinate system (EPSG:4326).‡CHM; Canopy Height Model; Lidar; PANGAEA; UAV; canopy cover; mountain native forest; temperate forests‡Dataset‡https://doi.pangaea.de/10.1594/PANGAEA.993863?format=textfile‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡-41.63007‡-71.8078‡-41.62084‡-71.78794‡Start Date: 2024-01-01 ; End Date: 2024-12-31

column7: Ulrike Herzschuh‡PANGAEA‡Field investigations were performed in the central Lena Delta. In total
column8: 25 sites were investigated. The sites were chosen to represent a variety of vegetation communities of the investigated region. 30 m² sample plots (sites) were demarcated in the most homogeneous locations. Heterogeneity was accommodated by roughly assorting vegetation into two to three vegetation types per sampling plot. Within each area of roughly estimated vegetation types we selected one 0.5 x 0.5 m subplot for representative ground-layer above-ground biomass (ABG) harvesting (major taxa and other). For moss and lichen AGB harvesting inside 0.5 x 0.5 m subplots representative 0.1 x 0.1 m subplots were chosen. All harvested AGB samples were weighed fresh in the field. In general
column9: AGB samples with a weight of more than 15 g were subsampled. All samples were oven dried (60 °C
column10: 24 h and weighed again). All ground-layer vegetation AGB assessments were calculated for the fifteen-meter radius plot in g/m² for each sample plot

column5: Thomas Böhmer‡PANGAEA‡"This data set presents the reconstructed vegetation cover for 446 Asian sites based on harmonized pollen data from the data set LegacyPollen 2.0. Sugita's REVEALS model (2007) was applied to all pollen records using REVEALSinR from the DISQOVER package (Theuerkauf et al. 2016). Pollen counts were translated into vegetation cover by accounting for taxon-specific pollen productivity and fall speed. Additionally
column6: relevant source areas of pollen were calculated using the aforementioned taxon-specific parameters and a Gaussian plume model for deposition and dispersal. Values for relative pollen productivity and fall speed from the synthesis from Wiezcorek and Herzschuh (2010) were updated with recent studies used to reconstruct vegetation cover. The average values from all Northern Hemisphere values were used where taxon-specific continental values were unavailable. As REVEALS was conceived to reconstruct vegetation from large lakes
column7: only records originating from large lakes (>= 50h) are marked as ""valid as site"" in the dataset. Reconstructions from other records can be used when spatially averaging several together. An example script to do so is provided on Zenodo (https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.12800290). Reconstructed tree cover was validated using modern Landsat remote sensing forest cover. Reconstructed tree cover has much lower errors than the original arboreal pollen percentages. Reconstructions of individual taxa are more uncertain. We present tables with reconstructed vegetation cover for all continents with original parameters. As further details
column8: we list a table with the taxon-specific parameters used
column10: and a list of parameters adjusted in the default version of REVEALSinR."‡PANGAEA; REVEALS; fossil pollen; paleoecology‡Dataset‡https://download.pangaea.de/dataset/963610/files/2025_Asia.csv‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡18.377‡-170.248‡75.33333‡44.47‡

column6: Peter Ewald‡PANGAEA‡Vegetation surveys were carried out in Northwest Territories
column7: Yukon and British Columbia in Canada. The study area is within the boreal forest biome and is partially underlain by permafrost soils. The aim was to record the projective ground vegetation in different boreal forest types studied during the CA-Land_2022_NWCanada field campaign in July and August 2022. The ground vegetation projective cover in percent was assessed within a circular forest plot of 15m radius. Depending on the heterogeneity of the forest plot
column10: or VC) were surveyed separately. The assignment of a vegetation type is always unique to a site. Up to four quadrats of 2x2 m were surveyed per vegetation type and projective cover in percent recorded separately for herbaceous and moss layers. All vegetation smaller than 40cm was recorded. Additionally
column11: ground vegetation projective cover was surveyed in 4 rings of 50 cm width around the center of the circular forest plot. Average projective cover per plot was calculated by using an average weighted by vegetation types for each site. The ring survey data was not included in the plot average. In total 32 forest plots were investigated. All data were collected by scientists from the Alfred Wegener Institute

column5: Lasse Riemann‡PANGAEA‡This dataset contains results from incubation experiments investigating the impact of sediment resuspension on nitrogen (N₂) fixation in a microtidal fjord environment. Experiments were conducted seasonally during June
column8: and September 2023 using water and sediment from Kærby Fed in Odense Fjord
column9: Denmark. The aim was to evaluate whether varying intensities of resuspension affect pelagic N₂ fixation activity. Measured parameters include: (i) N₂ fixation rates
column10: determined using the 15N₂ tracer method following Mohr et al. (2010)
column11: (ii) particulate organic carbon (POC) and particulate organic nitrogen (PON) content of the added sediment
column12: analyzed by Elemental Analysis–Isotope Ratio Mass Spectrometry
column13: (iii) concentrations of dissolved inorganic nitrogen (DIN) and phosphorus (DIP) in the overlying water
column14: measured using a spectrophotometer. Incubations involved sediment slurries subjected to different resuspension intensities
column15: simulating a range of bed-shear stress conditions. All treatments were conducted in triplicates
column16: and the dataset includes standard deviations for replicate measurements. The data support assessments of how sediment-driven processes influence microbial nitrogen cycling in coastal zones of the Danish Baltic Sea.‡Coastal dynamics; PANGAEA; Resuspension; diazotrophs; nitrogen cycling; nitrogen fixation; seasonal fluctuations‡Dataset‡https://doi.pangaea.de/10.1594/PANGAEA.983171?format=textfile‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡‡‡‡‡Start Date: 2023-06-19 ; End Date: 2023-09-05

column7: Anne E Todgham‡PANGAEA‡Amphipods (Gammarus sp.) were observed and collected from the Tule Red Tidal Restoration Site in Suisun Marsh (38.117843
column8: -121.983199) in January-December 2021. All observations and collections were conducted in <20 cm of brackish water in secondary tidal channels on the restoration site within 0.25 miles of the provided GPS coordinates. All amphipods were collected under California Scientific Collecting Permit No. S-201970002-20281-001. Monthly field observations and captures were done at 5 m intervals over eight semi-permanent 20 m transects established in paired Phragmites and native plant canopies in the same area of the marsh
column9: where 0 m indicated the edge of a tidal channel. We measured pelagic invertebrate biodiversity by capturing discrete water pools (~60 g water) using a turkey baster. The water samples were sieved using a 0.125 mm filter and rinsed with deionized water before preservation in a rose bengal-dyed 70% ethanol solution. We also measured benthic invertebrate biodiversity with a sediment mud core (5 cm diameter and 10 cm depth). The benthic samples were sieved and washed using a 0.5 mm filter and rinsed with deionized water before preservation in a rose bengal-dyed 70% ethanol solution. Preserved samples were sorted
column10: counted
column11: and photographed under a dissecting microscope. Counts per sample were normalized to the weight of the original sample. column7: standard deviations of EC and TP

column10: TP
column12: or located in different parts of lake. Additional metadata contain three columns: ""Species""
column13: ""Authority"" and ""Code"". Column ""Species"" includes names of diatom taxa. Column ""Authority"" contains information on authorities of taxa. column14: names of diatom species in fossil record should be replaced with the appropriate codes. We developed the transfer function for quantitative reconstructions of total phosphorus ranging from 5.6 to 313 μg l-1 and electric conductivity ranging from 61 to 21.700 μS/cm. WA-PLS-Component 2 (RMSEPboot=0.2 log10 μg/l
column15: bootstrap r2=0.6 log10 μg/l) was the best performing model for TP. WA with classical deshrinking (RMSEPboot=0.2 log10 μS/cm
column16: bootstrap r2=0.8) was the best performing model for EC. column14: Marc Strous‡PANGAEA‡Groundwater ecosystems are globally widespread yet still poorly understood. We investigated the microbiology of >100 groundwater samples from 90 monitoring wells (<250m depth) located in 14 aquifers in the Canadian Prairie. This dataset contains the nucleotide sequences and taxonomy of archaeal 16S rRNA gene amplicons retrieved from groundwater samples of aquifers in the Canadian Prairie
column15: collected between 2015 and 2020. The study area comprised 14 major aquifers and a geographic area of ~210.000 km2. The goal of the study was to understand the links between the biogeochemistry and microbial ecology of groundwater ecosystems in diverse geological settings on a broad spatial scale. Details concerning DNA extraction

column3: Anne F Sell‡PANGAEA‡Squids were collected during the research cruise SO285 on RV Sonne in the northern (nBUS) and southern (sBUS) Benguela Upwelling System between September 13 and October 9
column4: 2021 in order to determine their trophic position and nutritional ecology. The cruise was part of the TRAFFIC (Trophic tRAnsfer eFFICiency in the Benguela Current) project. Samples were retrieved from rectangular midwater trawl (RMT) net hauls at 10 stations in the nBUS (sampling ranged from surface down to 230–600 m) and 6 stations in the sBUS (sampling ranged from surface down to 500–555 m). Furthermore
column5: some squids were caught using hook and line in the upper two meters of the water column at 2 stations in the nBUS (ranging from 20 to 115 min per station
column6: 4–5 rods) and 4 stations in the sBUS (ranging from 45 to 105 min per station
column7: 4–6 rods). At 1 station in sBUS one squid was retrieved from a neuston catamaran haul (NCAT) used to sample the surface layer (0­–20 cm sampling depth). Dorsal mantle length (DML) of each specimen was measured to the nearest mm below. Mantle muscle tissue sample was taken from 82 squid specimens for stable isotope analyses (SIA). SIA revealed significant differences in δ15N values of small squids (dorsal mantle length <10 cm) between the two subsystems
column8: but none in those of large squids (dorsal mantle length >10 cm). Trophic position estimates based on Post (2002) ranged from 2.2 to 5.0. Isotopic niche width was widest in the families Ommastrephidae and Cranchiidae.‡Cephalopods; Food web; PANGAEA; nutritional ecology; trophic markers; trophic position; upwelling systems‡Dataset‡https://doi.pangaea.de/10.1594/PANGAEA.974072?format=textfile‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/‡-31.99282‡11.09808‡-20.04937‡15.84952‡Start Date: 2021-09-13 ; End Date: 2021-10-09
